narrative_blocks.header: He led Yale students against the British raid
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale student during the Revolution, George Welles commanded a band of fellow students who volunteered to oppose the British invasion of New Haven in 1779. He graduated in the class of 1779.

narrative_blocks.header: Land agent and innkeeper on the Pennsylvania frontier
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale, Welles settled at Athens on the Pennsylvania frontier as agent for the Carroll lands and proprietor of the Athens Hotel. He grew rich in valuable lands and served as a justice of the peace.

narrative_blocks.header: He died in the Sheshequin fever epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: In 1813 a deadly epidemic the settlers called the 'Sheshequin fever' swept Tioga Point, striking only adults and sparing no one under fifteen. George Welles was among its victims, buried in late June at the height of the outbreak.

bio.bio_blurb: Yale-educated Pennsylvania land agent and innkeeper

narrative_blocks.header: He led Yale students against the British raid
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale student during the Revolution, George Welles commanded a band of fellow students who volunteered to oppose the British invasion of New Haven in 1779. He graduated in the class of 1779.

narrative_blocks.header: Land agent and innkeeper on the Pennsylvania frontier
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale, Welles settled at Athens on the Pennsylvania frontier as agent for the Carroll lands and proprietor of the Athens Hotel. He grew rich in valuable lands and served as a justice of the peace.

narrative_blocks.header: He died in the Sheshequin fever epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: In 1813 a deadly epidemic the settlers called the 'Sheshequin fever' swept Tioga Point, striking only adults and sparing no one under fifteen. George Welles was among its victims, buried in late June at the height of the outbreak.

bio.bio_blurb: Yale-educated Pennsylvania land agent and innkeeper

narrative_blocks.header: The general who wrote Pennsylvania's Academy Bill
narrative_blocks.body: Elected to the Pennsylvania House in 1809 and the state Senate from 1815 to 1819, Henry Welles authored the 1813 Academy Bill to expand schooling across the commonwealth. Governor Simon Snyder named him an aide with the rank of general, the title by which everyone afterward knew him.

narrative_blocks.header: A township was named in his honor
narrative_blocks.body: A fluent orator said to charm any room or legislature he entered, Welles became one of the foremost makers of Athens, Pennsylvania. Wells Township was named for him.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife died weeks after the wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Henry married Phoebe Patrick in February 1809, but she died only weeks later, leaving him a young widower. Three years on he married Sarah 'Sally' Spalding, who would outlive him by forty-four years.

bio.photo_notes: Gen. Henry Welles (1781–1833), Pennsylvania state senator.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Welleses wed two Hollenbacks
narrative_blocks.body: Susan Welles married John Hollenback in 1805, and her brother Charles Fisher Welles later married John's sister Ellen, joining two of Judge Matthias Hollenback's children to two of George Welles's. Susan and John had no children of their own.

narrative_blocks.header: Bradford County's first clerk of courts
narrative_blocks.body: At twenty-three Charles Fisher Welles was appointed the first prothonotary and clerk of the courts of Bradford County, holding the offices six years. His court records were long admired as models of neatness and accuracy.

narrative_blocks.header: A diffident poet with a trenchant pen
narrative_blocks.body: A lifelong lover of poetry who studied law but was too shy to practice it, Welles instead wielded a busy pen for the press. His political essays were said to carry conviction to the reader and scathing rebuke to the corrupt politician.

narrative_blocks.header: A fall from his wagon killed him
narrative_blocks.body: Welles kept a hand in public affairs for half a century and rarely missed a term of court at Towanda. In 1866, getting down from his wagon, he was thrown heavily and died of his injuries.

bio.photo_notes: Charles Fisher Welles (1789–1866), first prothonotary of Bradford County.
bio.bio_blurb: Bradford County's first prothonotary and clerk

bio.photo_notes: Eleanor Jones "Ellen" Hollenback Welles (1788–1876), daughter of Judge Matthias Hollenback.

narrative_blocks.header: The adopted son who built the railroads
narrative_blocks.body: Adopted by Charles Fisher Welles and given his name, this younger Charles F. Welles grew into a prominent man of enterprise. Northern Pennsylvania and western New York were said to owe much to his railroad-building before his sudden death in the autumn of 1873.

bio.bio_blurb: Pennsylvania railroad developer; adopted Welles son

narrative_blocks.header: She preserved the family's Woodbridge legend
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Welles Pumpelly handed down the cherished story of her great-great-grandmother Jerusha Pitkin, the English lady whom the gentlemen of Hartford schemed to keep in the colony. Through her telling the tale of the ancestress of the Woodbridges was preserved as authentic family history.

narrative_blocks.header: Raphael Pumpelly's brother who settled in Massachusetts
narrative_blocks.body: John Hollenbeck Pumpelly, an elder brother of the geologist Raphael Pumpelly, was born at Owego, New York, in 1826. He married Mary Ann Foote in 1868 and died at Waltham, Massachusetts, in 1907.

notable.notable_blurb: Geologist and explorer of Asia's interior

narrative_blocks.header: He mapped Asia's deserts for American science
narrative_blocks.body: Trained as a mining engineer, Raphael Pumpelly surveyed mineral country across Japan, China, and Mongolia in the 1860s and crossed the Gobi Desert. His later expeditions into Russian Central Asia searched for the origins of agriculture and early civilization.

narrative_blocks.header: He tied windblown dust to climate history
narrative_blocks.body: Pumpelly's studies of loess, the fine wind-laid soil of northern China, helped show how such deposits record ancient climate. At home he directed the northern transcontinental railroad survey and led major government geological surveys.

narrative_blocks.header: He held Harvard's first chair in mining
narrative_blocks.body: From 1866 to 1875 Pumpelly served as Harvard's first professor of mining, between expeditions that carried him around the world. He later organized the economic-geology division of the new U.S. Geological Survey and directed its New England branch.

narrative_blocks.header: His Anau dig pushed Central Asian history back
narrative_blocks.body: In 1903 Pumpelly led a Carnegie-funded expedition to the Anau mounds in Turkmenistan, uncovering a settlement he dated to about 4500 BCE. The mineral pumpellyite, found in Michigan's copper country, was later named in his honor.

narrative_blocks.header: He blazed the Pumpelly Trail up Monadnock
narrative_blocks.body: From his summer place at Dublin, New Hampshire, Pumpelly cut a ridge trail to the summit of Mount Monadnock that still carries his name. He spent his last decades at Newport and presided over the Geological Society of America in 1905.

cross_connections.display_label: descends from Springfield's founder William Pynchon
descends from Connecticut governor Thomas Welles
the mineralogist in whose system pumpellyite is classed

bio.photo_notes: John Welles of Glastonbury, husband of Jerusha Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed by a mislabeled medicine bottle
narrative_blocks.body: Feeling unwell, Jerusha Welles was advised to take a small dose of variolated tartar. A wrong label led her to swallow corrosive sublimate of mercury instead, and she died within days in 1778, leaving five children and a reputation for piety and charity.
